meta.name: SCOFF Eating Disorder Screen
meta.description: The SCOFF is a 5-item rapid screening tool for identifying possible eating disorder risk, including anorexia nervosa and bulimia nervosa.
meta.author: Morgan JF, Reid F, Lacey JH (1999)
meta.reference: Morgan JF, Reid F, Lacey JH. The SCOFF questionnaire: assessment of a new screening tool for eating disorders. BMJ. 1999;319(7223):1467-1468.
meta.instruction: Please answer the following questions about your eating behaviors with Yes or No.

questions.text: Do you make yourself Sick because you feel uncomfortably full?
Do you worry you have lost Control over how much you eat?
Have you recently lost more than One stone (14 lbs / 6 kg) in a 3-month period?
Do you believe yourself to be Fat when others say you are too thin?
Would you say that Food dominates your life?

ranges.label: Low Risk
ranges.description: Your score suggests low risk for an eating disorder. Your responses do not indicate significant concerns about eating behaviors, body image, or compensatory behaviors. Maintaining a healthy relationship with food and your body is important for overall well-being.
suggestions: Continue maintaining a balanced approach to eating and physical activity
Be aware of cultural and social pressures regarding body image
Promote positive body image and intuitive eating practices

ranges.label: Possible Eating Disorder
ranges.description: Your score of 2 or more on the SCOFF indicates possible eating disorder symptoms that warrant further evaluation. You may be experiencing concerns about your eating behavior, body weight, or shape that could indicate an eating disorder such as anorexia nervosa, bulimia nervosa, or binge eating disorder.
suggestions: Seek evaluation from a healthcare professional experienced in eating disorders
Early intervention significantly improves outcomes for eating disorders
Eating disorders are serious but treatable medical conditions, not lifestyle choices
Evidence-based treatments include cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT-E), family-based therapy, and nutritional counseling
If you are restricting food intake severely or purging, seek medical evaluation for potential physical complications
